findings.description: The entire email canvas/grid system (600px width, 480px breakpoint, 12-col grid, 24px section padding, 8px vertical rhythm base, section top/bottom padding, and element gap spacing) is described in explicit, quantified, hard-constraint language but has zero corresponding candidate rules. Only the CTA button units (u_0008) were extracted; a large chunk of the source content is unrepresented, so the clustering for this doc_ref is incomplete rather than just mis-split.

payload.note: Add rules for: fixed 600px email width/centering, 480px mobile breakpoint with single-column collapse, 12-column internal grid layout options, 24px section side padding, 8px vertical rhythm base unit, 32px section top/bottom padding with zero-gap stacking, and specific element gaps (heading→body 16px, paragraph→paragraph 16px, body→CTA 24px, image→caption 8px).

findings.description: Source explicitly tags this CTA spec as '[GENERAL, contrast-checked to palette]', meaning it is not an official brand-guide directive but an inferred/baseline convention. The candidate rule carries hardness=hard_constraint with no governance facet capturing this provenance distinction, which risks the rule being treated as canonically mandated when the source flags it as general/inferred.

governance.note: Tagged [GENERAL, contrast-checked to palette] in source; not an explicit brand-guide mandate.

findings.description: The section heading marks the whole cluster as '[GENERAL — not specified in guide; Solstice email baseline]', which applies to the one-primary-CTA rule too, but no governance/provenance facet is attached indicating this is a baseline convention rather than a documented brand standard.

The rule bundles many distinct facets (primary color/text, font, padding, min touch target accessibility rule, corner radius, and a fully separate secondary CTA variant) into a single rule. The 48px minimum touch target is explicitly called out as a separate 'brand accessibility rule' and could warrant its own rule/relation rather than being flattened into one CTA spec, since it has distinct governance weight (accessibility mandate) vs purely stylistic specs (color, radius).
